tasks.title: Implement Go to Line dialog and navigation (Ctrl+G)
tasks.description: Add a Go to Line feature with a modal dialog that jumps the cursor to a specified line and centers it in the editor viewport.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Open a file with at least 100 lines.
- Press Ctrl+G and verify a modal dialog appears with current line as placeholder.
- Enter a valid line number (e.g., 50) and press Enter: cursor moves to that line and it is centered in the viewport.
- Enter line 1 and verify cursor jumps to the first line.
- Enter a line number greater than total lines: verify cursor goes to the last line.
- Hit Escape: dialog closes without moving the cursor.
- Click outside dialog: dialog closes.
- Verify shortcut does not trigger when focus is not in the editor (e.g., in settings).

tasks.title: Implement Duplicate Line / Selection (Ctrl+Shift+D)
tasks.description: Add a keyboard shortcut to duplicate the current line or the current selection, preserving cursor position relative to duplicated content.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Place cursor on a non-empty line with no selection; press Ctrl+Shift+D and verify the full line appears duplicated directly below, including indentation, and cursor remains on the original line with same column.
- Repeat with the cursor in the middle/end of the line; verify relative cursor position is preserved.
- Select a word on a line; press Ctrl+Shift+D and verify that word appears immediately after the original selection and remains selected.
- Select multiple lines and partial lines; press Ctrl+Shift+D and verify the exact selection is duplicated after itself.
- Verify undo reverts the duplication in a single step and redo reapplies it.
- Verify shortcut does nothing (or is ignored) when editor is read-only if such mode exists.

tasks.title: Implement Move Line Up/Down (Alt+↑/↓)
tasks.description: Allow users to move the current line or selected block of lines up or down with Alt+Up and Alt+Down, maintaining selection and respecting document boundaries.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- With cursor on a middle line, press Alt+Up and Alt+Down repeatedly; verify the line swaps position with adjacent lines and does not move past top or bottom.
- Select three consecutive lines; press Alt+Down and ensure the block moves down one line as a unit, with selection preserved over the moved block.
- Move selected lines up to the top of the document and verify they stop moving further.
- Move selected lines down to the bottom and verify they stop.
- Verify indentation and line contents are unchanged except for reordering.
- Confirm undo/redo restores original ordering in single steps.

tasks.title: Implement Auto-close Brackets & Quotes with configurable setting
tasks.description: Add automatic insertion of closing brackets and quotes while typing, including selection wrapping and skip-over behavior, controlled by an editor setting auto_close_brackets.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Enable auto-close in settings; type (, [, {, ", ', ` and verify the closing character is inserted and cursor is between them.
- Select a word (e.g., hello), type ( and verify it becomes (hello); repeat for [, {, ", ', `, **, and __.
- With `()` already present and cursor before `)`, type ) and ensure the cursor jumps over the existing ) instead of inserting another.
- Repeat skip-over behavior for ], }, ", ', and `.
- Disable auto-close in settings and verify typing brackets/quotes behaves as normal with no auto insertion or wrapping.
- Restart the app and verify existing config.json without the new field still loads, defaulting auto_close_brackets to true.
- Verify undo/redo works for auto-close operations as expected.

tasks.title: Implement Smart Paste for Links and images
tasks.description: Enhance paste behavior so that pasting a URL on selected text creates a markdown link, and pasting an image URL with no selection inserts a markdown image reference.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Type "Click here", select it, copy `https://example.com`, and paste: verify the result is `[Click here](https://example.com)` and cursor is after the closing parenthesis.
- Select partial text in the middle of a sentence and paste a URL; confirm markdown link syntax is correct and surrounding text is unchanged.
- With no selection, paste `https://example.com` and verify the plain URL is inserted.
- With no selection, paste an image URL like `https://example.com/pic.png` and verify `![](https://example.com/pic.png)` is inserted.
- Paste non-URL text with and without selection and verify normal paste behavior.
- Undo and redo each scenario to ensure edits are grouped logically.
- Test with URLs containing query strings and fragments to ensure detection still works.

tasks.title: Add Configurable Line Width setting and apply in all editor views
tasks.description: Introduce a max_line_width editor setting and use it to limit and center the text column in Raw, Rendered, Split, and Zen mode views.
tasks.details:
Target files: src/config/settings.rs, src/ui/settings.rs, src/editor/widget.rs, src/markdown/editor.rs.

Config model:
- In src/config/settings.rs:
  - Add a field, e.g., `pub max_line_width: MaxLineWidth`, where MaxLineWidth is an enum such as:
    ```rust
    pub enum MaxLineWidth {
        Off,
        Col80,
        Col100,
        Col120,
        Pixels(u32),
    }
    ```
  - Implement serde (Serialize/Deserialize) with sensible defaults (Off) and backward-compatible tags.
  - Optionally support legacy numeric values mapping to Pixels.

Settings UI:
- In src/ui/settings.rs:
  - Add a dropdown under Editor for "Maximum line width" with options: Off, 80 characters, 100 characters, 120 characters, Custom pixel width.
  - When Custom pixel width is chosen, show a numeric input field (pixels) to set the value.
  - On change, update in-memory settings and trigger a UI refresh (e.g., via a message to editor views).

Editor layout logic:
- In src/editor/widget.rs (raw/split views):
  - Convert `max_line_width` to a target column width if using monospace fonts: 80/100/120 characters correspond to a clamped layout width in pixels: width_px = char_width * columns.
  - For Pixels(u32), use the provided pixel width directly.
  - Compute available viewport width; if max_line_width is Off or width_px >= viewport width, keep existing behavior.
  - Otherwise:
    - Center the text column horizontally by applying horizontal padding: left_padding = (viewport_width - width_px) / 2.
    - Apply a layout constraint so text content wraps at the target width (e.g., configure text layout engine’s max_width).
- In src/markdown/editor.rs (rendered view):
  - Apply the same width logic to the rendered markdown column, including Mermaid diagrams and other blocks, ensuring they respect the max width where possible.
  - Use a container widget with fixed max width and center alignment.
- Ensure Zen mode also uses the same width calculation, possibly with a different default padding but honoring the setting.
- Provide a way to listen to settings changes (observer or message) so changing the setting results in immediate layout update without restart.

Pseudo-code (layout):
```rust
fn layout(&mut self, viewport_width: f32) {
    let width_px = match settings.max_line_width {
        MaxLineWidth::Off => viewport_width,
        MaxLineWidth::Col80 => char_w * 80.0,
        MaxLineWidth::Col100 => char_w * 100.0,
        MaxLineWidth::Col120 => char_w * 120.0,
        MaxLineWidth::Pixels(px) => px as f32,
    };
    let content_width = width_px.min(viewport_width);
    let left_pad = (viewport_width - content_width) / 2.0;
    self.inner.set_max_width(content_width);
    self.inner.set_margin_left(left_pad);
}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- In Settings > Editor, set max line width to Off and verify current behavior is unchanged in all views.
- Set to 80 characters and open a document with long lines; verify text wraps at approximately 80 columns and the text column is centered in Raw view.
- Switch to Rendered view and Split view; verify the same width and centering behavior.
- Toggle between 80, 100, and 120 characters and confirm the column width changes immediately without restart.
- Choose Custom pixel width (e.g., 600 px) and confirm visual column width corresponds approximately to that size.
- Enter an extreme custom value (small and large) and ensure layout remains usable.
- Enter Zen mode and ensure it respects the max_line_width setting.
- Restart the app and verify the saved setting is persisted and applied on startup.

tasks.title: Add Linux musl static build job to CI
tasks.description: Configure the release CI workflow to build a statically-linked Linux binary using musl libc targeting x86_64-unknown-linux-musl.
tasks.details:
Target file: .github/workflows/release.yml.

Implementation outline:
- In the GitHub Actions workflow for releases, add a new job named `build-linux-musl`.
- Use either the rust-musl-builder Docker image or install the musl target directly.

Option A – rust-musl-builder container (simple, common approach):
- Use a job step like:
  ```yaml
  build-linux-musl:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    container:
      image: ekidd/rust-musl-builder:stable
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build release binary
        run: |
          cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
      - name: Package artifact
        run: |
          mkdir -p dist
          cp target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/ferrite dist/ferrite
          tar -czf ferrite-linux-musl-x64.tar.gz -C dist ferrite
      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: ferrite-linux-musl-x64
          path: ferrite-linux-musl-x64.tar.gz
  ```

Option B – install musl target:
- Add steps:
  - `rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl`.
  - Install musl tools (`musl-tools` on Ubuntu) if needed.
  - Build with `cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl`.

- Integrate the new artifact into the release packaging step (e.g., attach to GitHub Releases if existing workflow does this).
- Ensure no new Rust crates are added per PRD (only toolchain/image changes).
tasks.testStrategy:
Validation steps:
- Trigger the workflow on a test branch or draft release and verify the `build-linux-musl` job passes.
- Download the ferrite-linux-musl-x64.tar.gz artifact from CI.
- In a local or CI-run Alpine Linux container:
  - Extract the tarball and run `./ferrite --help` (or equivalent) to ensure the binary starts.
- In an Ubuntu container or host:
  - Run the same binary and ensure it starts without missing shared library errors (use `ldd ferrite` or equivalent to verify static linking).
- Confirm the artifact is included alongside other release artifacts on tagged builds.

tasks.title: Fix Linux close button cursor flicker in custom window frame
tasks.description: Investigate and resolve cursor flicker near the close button on Linux by adjusting hit-testing and cursor handling for the custom window frame.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests on Linux:
- On Linux Mint (or similar distro), move the mouse slowly around the close button area and verify the cursor remains stable, without rapidly switching between pointer, resize, and move icons.
- Click the close button to ensure the window still closes as expected.
- Test resizing the window from edges and corners to ensure resize cursors still appear and resizing works.
- Test dragging the window from the title bar area to ensure move behavior remains intact.
- Regression check on other platforms (Windows, macOS) to confirm cursor behavior is unchanged or improved and no new flicker is introduced.

tasks.title: Optimize Mermaid rendering performance with caching
tasks.description: Profile and optimize Mermaid diagram rendering for complex diagrams by caching computations based on source hash and avoiding unnecessary recomputation.
tasks.details:
Target file: src/markdown/mermaid.rs.

Implementation outline:
- Profiling and hotspots:
  - Add lightweight timing instrumentation around parsing, layout, and rendering functions (e.g., using std::time::Instant and logging in debug builds).
  - Test with synthetic Mermaid diagrams containing 50+ nodes to identify the main bottlenecks.
- Caching design:
  - Define a cache keyed by a hash of the Mermaid source string (e.g., using a fast hash like FxHash or std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher).
  - Cache structure may be:
    ```rust
    struct MermaidCacheEntry {
        layout: LayoutData,
        render: RenderData,
    }
    ```
  - Store entries in a HashMap<String, MermaidCacheEntry> or an LRU cache if memory is a concern.
- Render pipeline changes:
  - On rendering a mermaid code block:
    - Compute hash of the source text.
    - If present in cache and source unchanged, reuse cached layout/render data and skip recomputation.
    - If not present or source changed, run full parse + layout + render preparation, then store result in cache.
  - Ensure cache is scoped appropriately (per-document or global) and invalidated when diagrams are edited.
- Algorithmic optimizations:
  - Parsing:
    - Reuse token buffers; avoid allocations by using pre-allocated Vecs and `clear()` instead of creating new ones.
    - Avoid repeated string cloning; use &str slices into the original source.
  - Layout:
    - Replace O(N²) loops where possible with more efficient graph traversal or adjacency lists.
    - Cache intermediate computations (e.g., node sizes, edge routes) across rerenders of identical diagrams.
  - Rendering:
    - Batch draw calls for nodes and edges when using the graphics backend (e.g., combine into a single path where possible).
    - Reuse shape objects or text layouts for identical nodes.

Pseudo-code:
```rust
fn render_mermaid(src: &str) -> RenderOutput {
    let key = hash_src(src);
    if let Some(entry) = CACHE.lock().unwrap().get(&key) {
        return render_from_cached(&entry.render);
    }
    let parsed = parse_diagram(src, &mut parser_bufs);
    let layout = compute_layout(&parsed, &mut layout_bufs);
    let render_data = prepare_render_data(&layout, &mut render_bufs);
    CACHE.lock().unwrap().insert(key.clone(), MermaidCacheEntry { layout, render: render_data.clone() });
    render_from_cached(&render_data)
}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
Performance tests:
- Create or use an existing Mermaid diagram with 50+ nodes and multiple edges.
- Measure and log render time before changes (cold render and subsequent renders) in debug builds.
- After implementing caching and optimizations, re-measure render time.
  - Confirm significant reduction, especially on repeated renders where the source has not changed.
- Edit a diagram (change text slightly) and verify the cache invalidates and recomputes only for the modified block.

Functional tests:
- Compare screenshots or visual output of diagrams before and after optimization to ensure no visual regression.
- Test different diagram types (flowchart, sequence, class, etc.) to ensure all still render correctly.
- Run the existing test suite (if present) and add targeted unit tests for caching logic, ensuring correct hits/misses and no stale data usage.

tasks.title: Cleanup and modularize Mermaid codebase
tasks.description: Refactor Mermaid implementation to reduce warnings, remove dead code, and optionally split into parser/layout/render submodules while preserving behavior.
tasks.details:
Target file(s): src/markdown/mermaid.rs → potentially src/markdown/mermaid/.

Implementation outline:
- Run static analysis:
  - Execute `cargo clippy` focusing on the mermaid module and note warnings (unused code, needless clones, etc.).
  - Execute `cargo doc` to ensure public API is documented.
- Code cleanup:
  - Remove dead code that is verifiably unused; if certain code must remain for future features, mark with `#[allow(dead_code)]` and add a comment explaining why.
  - Address common Clippy warnings: prefer slices over Vec where possible, avoid unnecessary clones, use iterators idiomatically.
  - Add `///` documentation comments to public functions/types explaining their purpose and behavior.
- Optional modularization:
  - If src/markdown/mermaid.rs is large and monolithic, split into submodules:
    - src/markdown/mermaid/mod.rs
    - src/markdown/mermaid/parser.rs
    - src/markdown/mermaid/layout.rs
    - src/markdown/mermaid/render.rs
  - Move corresponding functions and types into each file.
  - Update `mod` declarations and pub use statements to preserve the external API.
- Ensure no new dependencies are introduced; use only std and existing crates.
- Keep tests and benchmarks (if any) in sync with new module structure.
tasks.testStrategy:
Validation steps:
- Run `cargo clippy` and verify there are no warnings in the mermaid module (or only explicitly allowed ones with comments).
- Run `cargo doc` and ensure documentation builds without errors and public items in the mermaid module have clear descriptions.
- Run the full test suite to ensure no regressions.
- Manually open various Mermaid diagrams in the app and verify they render as before after the refactor.
- If modularization was done, confirm that external callers (markdown renderer, toolbar, etc.) still compile and function without changes to their usage patterns.

tasks.title: Implement Mermaid diagram insertion toolbar with templates
tasks.description: Add a "Diagram" or "Mermaid" button to the ribbon that provides a dropdown of Mermaid diagram templates and inserts fenced code blocks at the editor cursor.
tasks.details:
Target file: src/ui/ribbon.rs (plus any template storage).

Implementation outline:
- Ribbon UI:
  - In src/ui/ribbon.rs, add a new button group labeled "Diagram" or "Mermaid".
  - Attach a dropdown menu listing the template types: Flowchart, Sequence, Class, State, ER, Gantt, Pie, Mindmap, Timeline, Git Graph, User Journey.
- Template storage:
  - Define a static in-code map or enum with associated template strings for each type, e.g.:
    ```rust
    struct Template { name: &'static str, snippet: &'static str }
    ```
  - Example for Flowchart as given in PRD:
    ```
    ```mermaid
    flowchart TD
        A[Start] --> B{Decision}
        B -->|Yes| C[Action]
        B -->|No| D[End]
    ```
    ```
  - Ensure escape sequences are correct in Rust string literals (use raw strings `r"""` where needed).
- Insertion behavior:
  - On selecting a template from the dropdown:
    - Send a message/event to the editor to insert the corresponding snippet at the current cursor position.
    - If there is a selection, optionally replace it or insert before/after; simplest is insertion at cursor, ignoring selection for now unless UX dictates otherwise.
  - Ensure insertion respects current indentation level if the editor has line-indentation logic.
- Integration with preview:
  - Since diagrams are already supported, simply ensure the inserted ` ```mermaid` fenced block triggers the existing Mermaid renderer in preview.

Pseudo-code:
```rust
fn on_template_selected(kind: TemplateKind) {
    let snippet = templates::get(kind);
    app.send(EditCommand::InsertText(snippet.to_string()));
}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Open a markdown document with the editor focused.
- Click the new "Diagram"/"Mermaid" button and choose "Flowchart"; verify a mermaid fenced code block with the example content is inserted at the cursor.
- Switch to Rendered or Split view and confirm the flowchart diagram renders correctly.
- Repeat for each template type (Sequence, Class, State, ER, Gantt, Pie, Mindmap, Timeline, Git Graph, User Journey) and ensure diagrams render or at least show valid Mermaid syntax.
- Test insertion at the start, middle, and end of a document.
- Test with existing text selected and verify insertion behavior is consistent and does not corrupt adjacent markdown.
- Verify undo/redo works for template insertions.

tasks.title: Add Mermaid syntax hints to Help panel
tasks.description: Extend the Help panel to include a Mermaid reference section listing supported diagram types, basic syntax examples, and a link reference to official Mermaid documentation.
tasks.details:
Target file: src/ui/about.rs (Help panel implementation).

Implementation outline:
- Help panel UI:
  - Locate the Help or About panel implementation in src/ui/about.rs.
  - Add a new section titled "Mermaid Diagrams" or similar.
- Content:
  - List supported diagram types: Flowchart, Sequence, Class, State, ER, Gantt, Pie, Mindmap, Timeline, Git Graph, User Journey.
  - For each type, add a concise fenced code example, e.g.:
    - Flowchart:
      ```
      ```mermaid
      flowchart TD
          A[Start] --> B[End]
      ```
      ```
    - Sequence:
      ```
      ```mermaid
      sequenceDiagram
          Alice->>Bob: Hello
      ```
      ```
    - etc., keeping examples short.
  - Include a text note such as "For full syntax, see the official Mermaid documentation"; present the URL as plain text rather than clickable if link widgets are not available.
- Layout considerations:
  - Use scrollable area if the content is long.
  - Apply monospace font styling to fenced code blocks for readability.
- Ensure keyboard shortcut (Ctrl+? or Help menu) still opens the panel and focuses content appropriately.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Open the Help panel via Ctrl+? or through the Help menu.
- Scroll to the Mermaid section and verify it is present with clearly labeled diagram types and corresponding syntax examples.
- Confirm that code blocks are readable and correctly formatted (backticks visible, indentation correct).
- Copy-paste an example from the Help panel into the editor and verify it renders as a Mermaid diagram in preview.
- Check that the rest of the Help content remains intact and visually consistent.
